exemplar.title: How does Sylvia Plath portray the protagonistâs depression in her novel âThe Bell Jarâ, in  the 1950s?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does Sylvia Plath portray the protagonistâs depression in her novel âThe Bell Jarâ, in  the 1950s?

subject.title: English B
subject.description:
**IB English B** is a **Group 2** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **language acquisition through five prescribed themes: identities, experiences, human ingenuity, social organization, and sharing the planet**, with a strong focus on **developing receptive, productive, and interactive communication skills in English as a second language**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Productive skills - writing | 1 hour | 1 hour 15 minutes | 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Receptive skills - listening and reading comprehension | 1 hour 45 minutes | 2 hours | 50% |
| **Individual Oral** | Internal assessment - oral communication | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 25% |

strengths: Precise and consistent literary focus on Esther Greenwoodâs depression within a 1950s context
Clearly stated and focused research question that drives each major section
Use of section headings and quotations that directly connect back to the research question
weaknesses: Methodology lacks clear explanation of source selection criteria and search strategy
Inconsistent citation practice and no explicit rationale for the âfree flow methodâ
Exceeds the 4 000-word limit and includes occasional digressions into general plot summary
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the topic and research question effectively, maintaining a coherent focus throughout. However, the methodology description is incomplete and citations are inconsistent. These strengths and weaknesses place the work at the lower end of the 5â6 band (5 for topic and question) but limited methodology brings the overall to a solid 4/6.

strengths: Use of primary quotations and secondary sources that are mostly relevant to mental-health history
Clear and coherent subject knowledge of plot events and historical context
Accurate definition and reuse of key psychological and literary terms
weaknesses: Uneven integration of sources with some factual statements unreferenced
Large narrative passages reduce analytical depth
Inconsistent citation formatting and occasional imprecise terminology
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear knowledge and generally appropriate application of source material but relies heavily on descriptive rather than analytical use. Citation inconsistencies and narrative bias place it firmly in the midâband for Knowledge and Understanding (3/6).

strengths: Adequate breadth of research with a variety of primary and secondary sources
Insightful analysis of contrasting treatments (e.g., Gordon vs. Nolan) and imagery supporting conclusions
A coherent argument about compassionate care that ties back to the research question
weaknesses: Reliance on popular rather than strictly scholarly sources limits research quality
Significant plot summary and surface-level evaluation in places
Limited counterarguments and occasional repetitive conclusions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work achieves an âadequateâ level of critical thinking, with clear relevance to the research question and some strong analytical moments. However, inconsistencies in source quality, depth of evaluation and overâreliance on summary restrict it to the lower end of the 4â6 band (4/12).

strengths: Logical and complete structure with a thorough table of contents and clear subheadings
Consistent use of internal numbering and generally correct layout conventions
Readable formatting that supports navigation through the report
weaknesses: Section numbering inconsistencies (e.g., repeated â3.â) confuse the structure
Inconsistent citation style and extended paragraphs that impair readability
Exceeds word limit slightly, affecting overall presentation polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is clearly appropriate and complete in structure and layout, meriting band 3â4. Minor numbering errors, citation inconsistencies and wordâcount issues justify a midâband score of 3/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question â âHow does Sylvia Plath portray the protagonistâs depressionâ¦?â â is sharply stated, narrow enough to guide investigation of a single novel and firmly connected to the ensuing discussion.  Headings, quotations and secondary references continually return to that question.  Occasional expansion into medical history shows slight breadth creep, so band 5 rather than 6 is most fitting.

Analytical sections (e.g., on Doctor Gordon vs. Doctor Nolan) interpret imagery and link events to 1950s attitudes, but large passages recount plot or summarise secondary articles.  Arguments occasionally lapse into assertion, and counter-arguments are rare.  This represents âadequateâ rather than âgoodâ depth, so 4 fits within the 4-6 range.

Primary quotations are generally well-chosen and relevant, and most secondary sources relate to mental-health history or gender roles of the 1950s.  Nevertheless, integration is uneven; several citations are generic web summaries, and some factual statements are left unreferenced.  This constitutes âmostly relevant and appropriate applicationâ, corresponding to band 3-4 (score 3).

Layout conventions (title page details, bibliography, internal headings, table formatting) are mostly correct and readable.  Citation style is inconsistent and the word count slightly exceeds 4 000, but the physical layout itself is sound, so band 3-4, score 3, is appropriate.

The student demonstrates clear knowledge of plot events, historical context and basic psychological ideas, organising material coherently.  However, large portions remain narrative rather than analytical, and the secondary literature is used descriptively.  This places the work solidly in band 3-4; a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

The report follows a logical structure with contents page, numbered sections and clear sub-headings, fulfilling all expected elements.  Paragraphing occasionally wanders, but overall organisation is effective, aligning with the 3-4 band; score 3 reflects some minor structural imbalance.

A variety of primary passages and around ten secondary sources underpin the investigation, showing adequate breadth.  However, many sources are popular rather than scholarly, and critical comparison between them is limited.  Relevance to the RQ is clear but not consistently sustained, matching the lower end of the 4-6 band (score 4).

The student states a precise literary focus â the portrayal of Esther Greenwoodâs depression in The Bell Jar within its 1950s context â and reiterates this throughout the essay.  The purpose (to âenlighten the reader about mental healthâ and examine societyâs treatment of depression) is explicit and consistently referenced in each major section, and the internal headings match the stated focus.  Although some material drifts into general plot summary, the overall communication of topic, period and perspective is clear and appropriate, meriting placement in the 5-6 band; minor digressions justify the lower end (5).

The essay provides a discernible line of reasoning â that compassionate care is key to recovery â and the conclusion returns to this.  Yet evaluation of evidence is surface-level, and some claims (e.g., Plathâs positive stance on ECT) are insufficiently substantiated.  Minor inconsistencies keep the work in the 4-6 âadequateâ band at score 4.

A basic methodology is implied (close textual analysis plus secondary historical sources) and a contents list is supplied, but there is no explicit explanation of search strategy, source selection or evaluation of reliability.  Citations are present yet inconsistent, and the essay exceeds the 4 000-word limit.  This partial but not comprehensive treatment aligns with band 3-4; score 3 reflects the weaker end.

Key terms such as âdepressionâ, âbehaviourismâ, âpsychodynamicsâ, âelectroconvulsive therapyâ are defined accurately and reused, but some technical phrasing (e.g., âfree-flow methodâ, âviscous symbolâ) is imprecise and reference formatting is inconsistent.  Overall usage is âmostly accurateâ, justifying band 3-4, score 3.

annotations.comment: Section numbering repeats â3.â twice in the contents list, which can confuse readers; revise numbering consistency in the structure.
Introduce the term âdepersonalizationâ with a brief definition in the main text before citing Lempa to strengthen clarity.
The contrast between public and private hospital treatment is examined thoughtfully, demonstrating good critical evaluation.
The portrayal of the second ECT session and Estherâs changed response is insightful and effectively supports the conclusion.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes how societal and medical factors shape Plathâs depiction of depression, reinforcing the research question.
The inclusion of the research question as a clear section header with precise wording is commendable and guides the entire investigation effectively.
The title is clearly stated at the start of the report, fulfilling the formatting requirement and immediately orienting the reader.
The word count of 4016 exceeds the 4000-word limit, which may impact the score under formatting and methodology completeness.
The table of contents is thorough and complete, demonstrating an effective and logical structure for navigation.
The introduction situates the novel within its historical context of 1950s attitudes toward depression, showing relevant subject knowledge.
The methodology section mentions a âfree flow methodâ without explaining its procedures or rationale; include a clear description of search strategy and selection criteria.
The sentence about doctors perceiving mental health as unique diseases contains an empty parenthesis; add the missing citation or remove the placeholder.
Definitions of behaviourism and psychodynamics are correctly included but the footnotes are inconsistently formatted; align with a single citation style.
The contextualisation of psychological theories in the novel is well chosen and shows clear application of source material.
The quote illustrating Estherâs electrocuted-nerves imagery is effectively chosen and supports analytical depth.
The historical detail on the Rosenberg case lacks a supporting citation for some factual claims; add a secondary source reference.
Integration of Estherâs doubt about her future demonstrates good analytical engagement with Plathâs portrayal of depression.
Paragraphs in section 3.1 could explicitly link evidence back to the research question to strengthen focus and coherence.
Citing Davis when discussing unprocessed grief is apt, but ensure the bibliographic entry matches the in-text citation formatting.
Linking Estherâs childhood grief to her depression shows clear conceptual understanding and effective use of theory.
The quotation from de Beauvoir is well integrated and deepens the socio-historical context of womenâs roles.
The long paragraph in section 4.1 could be split to improve readability and help the reader follow each point.
Section header 4.2âs numbering style is inconsistent with earlier headers; revise for uniform formatting.
The brief discussion of the psychiatrist encounter would benefit from deeper critical evaluation of gender dynamics in medical practice.
The citation of Cleghorn adds a valuable scholarly dimension, but compare its argument directly with Plathâs perspective for stronger analysis.
The in-text reference to Cleghorn lacks parentheses around the citation year; ensure consistency in citation style.
Use of a secondary source to contextualize electroshock therapy is effective and shows solid application of research quality.
The reference to Ugo Cerlettiâs early ECT lacks an explicit in-text citation; add a source to support this historical claim.
The depiction of nurse attitudes toward Esther powerfully illustrates dehumanization and shows excellent analytical insight.
Some sentences in section 4.5 are overly long and complex; consider simplifying sentence structure to improve clarity and reader engagement.
The conclusion largely repeats earlier points rather than offering new evaluative insight; consider adding a forward-looking critique or implication.
The report lacks any personal reflection on the research process; including evaluative reflection would strengthen engagement.
Bibliographic entries mix MLA, APA, and other styles inconsistently; unify the citation format throughout.
